title: Moe Goes From Rags To Riches

jokes.text: And that's why I really don't believe there's a God. Thank you, and God bless America. You were only supposed to lead us in the Pledge of Allegiance. I'm pretty sure it was in there somewhere.
jokes.setup: Character was asked to lead the Pledge of Allegiance
jokes.punchline: Instead delivers atheist rant, then claims it was part of the Pledge
jokes.explanation: Strong setup/punchline with good absurdist logic - character genuinely believes his atheist rant contained the Pledge

jokes.text: Bedbugs? Just why is there a bed in City Hall? Er, uh, meeting adjourned!
jokes.setup: City Hall being fumigated for bedbugs
jokes.punchline: Logical question about why there's a bed in government building causes immediate adjournment
jokes.explanation: Solid observational humor exposing municipal corruption through simple logical question

jokes.text: This is so convenient! I can go straight from doing my civic duty to having a beer with my best friend Carl. And I can toss back a Duff Sangre de los Muertos with my best friend: Mexican Duffman. Ho-la!
jokes.setup: Homer and Lenny celebrating with their best friends after civic meeting
jokes.punchline: Lenny's best friend is Mexican Duffman, a corporate mascot variant
jokes.explanation: Character-specific humor that plays on Lenny's simplicity and Springfield's corporate absurdity

jokes.text: Hey, Moe, who's your best friend? Uh... Well, uh... Well, I just made friends with Pepto-Bismol on Facebook, and, uh... um...
jokes.setup: Direct question about Moe's friendships
jokes.punchline: Moe's pathetic admission that he befriended a stomach medicine on social media
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe character moment - desperately sad but specific to his lonely, pathetic persona

jokes.text: I think Moe's best friend is really that bar rag. That's even sadder than being friends with Milhouse!
jokes.setup: Homer observing Moe's relationship with his bar rag
jokes.punchline: Bart's comparative scale of sadness with Milhouse as the baseline
jokes.explanation: Efficient character humor that establishes both Moe's pathetic state and Bart's casual cruelty toward Milhouse

jokes.text: That's even sadder than being friends with Milhouse!
jokes.setup: Moe's friendship with bar rag is pathetic
jokes.punchline: Being friends with inanimate object is worse than being friends with Milhouse
jokes.explanation: Brutal burn that uses established character (Milhouse) as baseline for pathetic friendship. Efficient and quotable.

jokes.text: You know something, Bart? I'm getting tired of things like that. Tired of what? I dump on you, and you take it. That's how friendship works. Not anymore. Friendship over.
jokes.setup: Milhouse finally standing up to Bart's abuse
jokes.punchline: Bart's complete misunderstanding of friendship dynamics
jokes.explanation: Strong character development moment that's genuinely funny - Bart's casual admission of how he views friendship

jokes.text: What gives? He's not crawling back. Even a kid who wears a 'Finding Nemo' back brace has some pride.
jokes.setup: Bart expecting Milhouse to return
jokes.punchline: Specific, cruel observation about Milhouse's medical equipment
jokes.explanation: Perfectly cruel Bart observation with great specificity - Finding Nemo back brace is both pathetic and oddly endearing

jokes.text: Hey, Moe! I got a job here for your 'best friend,' the bar rag. Me, too! Witty.
jokes.setup: Homer and Carl making jokes about Moe's bar rag
jokes.punchline: Moe's deadpan 'Witty' response to their obvious mockery
jokes.explanation: Solid deadpan response from Moe showing his awareness of being mocked but powerlessness to stop it

jokes.text: Yes, that's right, everyone laugh at the rag. But I was not always this be-stainèd swatch you see before you. Oh, no, gentles. We begin in early medieval France...
jokes.setup: Bar rag responding to mockery
jokes.punchline: Rag begins elaborate historical narration in elevated language
jokes.explanation: Brilliant premise setup - inanimate object with grandiose backstory narrated in mock-epic style

jokes.text: That's not your estomacs! It's some men speaking French!
jokes.setup: Children complaining about grumbling stomachs in French
jokes.punchline: Mother reveals the 'grumbling' is actually French soldiers approaching
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection joke that plays on the ambiguity of 'grumbling' sounds

jokes.text: Papa? He is no help at all. Marguerite, I leave to fight in Flanders! Stupid Flanders.
jokes.setup: Medieval setting with husband leaving for war
jokes.punchline: Anachronistic Simpsons reference to Ned Flanders
jokes.explanation: Perfect Simpsons callback that bridges medieval story with show's universe - 'Stupid Flanders' is iconic

jokes.text: Me healing the sick. Me killing the healthy. Me marrying my sister. A long winter where nothing happens. Me relaxing with friends. You know, the standard stuff.
jokes.setup: Duke listing images for his tapestry
jokes.punchline: Increasingly disturbing requests presented as routine, ending with casual 'standard stuff'
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating list joke that builds from noble to horrific to mundane, with perfect deadpan delivery

jokes.text: I could walk six paces that way... or I could kill them all right now!
jokes.setup: Duke encountering sheep blocking his path
jokes.punchline: Considers reasonable solution vs. immediate genocide with equal weight
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing the Duke's casual evil - treating minor inconvenience and mass murder as equivalent options

jokes.text: I am heartily sorry and confess all my sins. Absolved!
jokes.setup: Duke about to commit mass sheep murder
jokes.punchline: Gets instant absolution before even committing the sin
jokes.explanation: Sharp satire of medieval church corruption with efficient setup/punchline structure

jokes.text: Right, and I was the Gutenberg Bible. Maman, we have failed to grow. We've had too little food! Is it too little food or too much complaining?
jokes.setup: Rag's grandiose claims being dismissed, then return to medieval poverty
jokes.punchline: Mother's weary response to children's complaints about malnutrition
jokes.explanation: Solid character humor showing medieval parent's exhaustion with children's complaints about basic survival

jokes.text: Perhaps to the South of France. We're in the South of France!
jokes.setup: Child suggesting vacation to South of France
jokes.punchline: They already live in the South of France
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective geographic irony joke with good timing

jokes.text: What an age for prosthetics we live in.
jokes.setup: Medieval setting
jokes.punchline: Duke making anachronistically modern observation about prosthetics
jokes.explanation: Mild anachronism joke that breaks medieval illusion with modern sensibility

jokes.text: It's not what I expected. And yet... it's beautiful! But it's also not what I expected. Burn down their house!
jokes.setup: Duke evaluating the tapestry
jokes.punchline: Rapid emotional swing from appreciation to destruction over same concern
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing the Duke's arbitrary cruelty and inability to process conflicted emotions

jokes.text: Hey, let's show some French courage and beat up the corpse!
jokes.setup: Duke has died
jokes.punchline: French peasant's idea of courage is attacking someone already dead
jokes.explanation: Solid dark humor about cowardice masquerading as bravery

jokes.text: Oh! We're submitting that to France's Funniest Cave Paintings.
jokes.setup: Someone gets hurt during corpse-beating
jokes.punchline: Ralph's anachronistic reference to modern TV show format in medieval times
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph line - combines his innocent misunderstanding with absurd anachronism

jokes.text: Guys, it ain't working-- the door's too strong.
jokes.setup: Vikings attempting to raid monastery
jokes.punchline: Anticlimactic failure of legendary raiders against basic architecture
jokes.explanation: Good subversion of Viking mythology with modern casual defeat

jokes.text: Guys, it ain't working-- the door's too strong.
jokes.setup: Vikings attempt to break down cathedral door
jokes.punchline: Fearsome raiders defeated by door construction
jokes.explanation: Standard deflation humor. Mighty warriors stopped by mundane obstacle.

jokes.text: You just broke your vow of silence. It wasn't a vow. I just didn't want to talk to you.
jokes.setup: Monk apparently breaking religious vow
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was just personal dislike, not religious devotion
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of religious expectations with petty human reality

jokes.text: This Viking stuff is too dangerous. I'm going to become a gentleman art thief.
jokes.setup: Viking finding raiding difficult
jokes.punchline: Career change to more refined but equally criminal profession
jokes.explanation: Funny character moment about career pivot with good contrast between brutal and sophisticated crime

jokes.text: Well, I was doing fine. Warm glass of milk, some choice cuts from The Wriggles and I was out.
jokes.setup: Bart saying he couldn't sleep after their fight
jokes.punchline: Milhouse's detailed routine involving children's band
jokes.explanation: Perfect Milhouse character moment - childish bedtime routine that's both pathetic and endearing

jokes.text: Bart, I'm not your puppet. I know, I made you into a real boy last week. And I'll always be grateful.
jokes.setup: Milhouse asserting independence
jokes.punchline: Reference to previous episode with Pinocchio joke
jokes.explanation: Solid callback that works as both character development and meta-reference to show continuity

jokes.text: I know, I made you into a real boy last week. And I'll always be grateful.
jokes.setup: Milhouse says he's not a puppet
jokes.punchline: Reference to Pinocchio story as if it actually happened between them
jokes.explanation: Clever literary reference that both acknowledges the puppet metaphor and creates fake shared history.

jokes.text: You have taken our gold and jewels and given us this faded cloth?! That's right. And if anyone asked who swindled you, it was Christians. Remember that name: Christians.
jokes.setup: Bad trade deal in ancient Persia
jokes.punchline: Trader blaming Christians as scapegoat group
jokes.explanation: Dark historical humor about scapegoating with good setup/punchline structure

jokes.text: Eh. Throw her in the pit of boring wives.
jokes.setup: King evaluating wife's entertainment value
jokes.punchline: Casual disposal into categorized wife storage
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist concept - bureaucratic organization of discarded wives with casual cruelty

jokes.text: We never go out anymore. Are you even listening to me? My sister has a much bigger pit.
jokes.setup: Wives in pit of boring wives
jokes.punchline: They immediately prove their boring status with typical complaints
jokes.explanation: Brilliant follow-up joke that validates the categorization while providing typical boring wife dialogue

jokes.text: Dude, you've already discarded, like, 500 wives. I think it's weird that you're counting.
jokes.setup: King's excessive wife disposal
jokes.punchline: King's deflection that the counting is the weird part
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection joke where the king focuses on the wrong aspect of criticism

jokes.text: Okay, just a couple thoughts on your direction so far.
jokes.setup: Scheherazade beginning her story
jokes.punchline: King giving modern entertainment industry feedback
jokes.explanation: Solid anachronism joke treating ancient storytelling like modern script notes

jokes.text: Make that two thieves! A million thieves! Too many. Forty thieves.
jokes.setup: Scheherazade creating Ali Baba story
jokes.punchline: Real-time creative process with king as editor leading to canonical number
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-joke showing the creative process behind famous story elements with good escalation

jokes.text: Uh, it was cooler in the pit.
jokes.setup: Scheherazade freeing imprisoned wives
jokes.punchline: Ungrateful response focusing on temperature comfort
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan response that subverts heroic rescue with mundane complaint

jokes.text: For once, couldn't you just take a few minutes to let the previous story sink in?
jokes.setup: King immediately demanding another story
jokes.punchline: Scheherazade's exasperation with his attention span
jokes.explanation: Relatable meta-humor about audience impatience with good character frustration

jokes.text: Only cowards use blindfolds. I didn't know you were doing that!
jokes.setup: Executioner calling condemned man a coward
jokes.punchline: Reveals he wasn't even aware of the blindfold option
jokes.explanation: Solid reversal joke where bravado becomes ignorance

jokes.text: How's your neck, Your Lordship? No splinters or nuffin'? I fear the axe less than that filthy rag.
jokes.setup: Executioner being solicitous about execution comfort
jokes.punchline: Lord preferring death to touching the dirty rag
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy showing aristocratic fastidiousness even facing death

jokes.text: Hey, you want a quickie? You go to Raphael, baby.
jokes.setup: Being rushed on the Sistine Chapel
jokes.punchline: Modern artist rivalry trash talk in Renaissance setting
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment giving Michelangelo modern artist attitude with historical accuracy

jokes.text: Now would you like to protect your investment with a clear coat?
jokes.setup: Sistine Chapel being completed
jokes.punchline: Renaissance master offering modern protective coating like house painter
jokes.explanation: Good anachronism reducing great art to commercial service transaction

jokes.text: No way I would abandon you, Raggie. You're my best friend.
jokes.setup: Homer suggesting Moe get a new rag
jokes.punchline: Moe's sincere emotional attachment to inanimate object
jokes.explanation: Perfect sad Moe moment that's both pathetic and touching in character

jokes.text: Gee, Bart, you seem, uh-- how can I put it?-- Milhousy. Really? It's not that bad, is it?
jokes.setup: Bart desperately trying to win back Milhouse
jokes.punchline: Bart's horror at being compared to Milhouse, the person he's trying to befriend
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment revealing Bart's fundamental inability to respect the person he needs

jokes.text: Not bad. Did Lisa write that? Yes, I did. Now I'm going to bed.
jokes.setup: Bart giving heartfelt apology speech
jokes.punchline: Immediate revelation Lisa wrote it, followed by her exit
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/revelation/exit sequence that perfectly captures all three characters

jokes.text: The glasses are off!
jokes.setup: Milhouse getting serious about ending friendship
jokes.punchline: Dramatic gesture of removing glasses as sign of business
jokes.explanation: Great Milhouse character moment - his version of 'getting serious' is removing glasses

jokes.text: Rag soup! Needs flavor.
jokes.setup: Depression-era poverty meal
jokes.punchline: Rag soup still being critiqued for taste
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor about maintaining standards even in extreme poverty

jokes.text: Come on, man. A few more balloons and this'll look really awesome. D'oh! Worst... climbing... Everest.
jokes.setup: Mountain climbing expedition in trouble
jokes.punchline: Sherpa focused on party decorations while Homer has classic frustrated response
jokes.explanation: Good character moment with Homer's classic reaction pattern plus absurd prioritization

jokes.text: Can I punch you? Sure. Can I have someone else punch you? Sure. What've you got?
jokes.setup: Bart desperately offering anything for forgiveness
jokes.punchline: Bart's willingness to accept increasingly absurd punishments
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Bart's desperation and Milhouse enjoying power reversal

jokes.text: Can I punch you? Sure. Can I have someone else punch you? Sure. What've you got? Puppy Goo-Goo coming my way?
jokes.setup: Bart desperately offers to be punched for forgiveness
jokes.punchline: Escalates to proxy violence, references embarrassing character
jokes.explanation: Good desperation escalation. The 'Puppy Goo-Goo' reference adds specific Simpsons flavor.

jokes.text: I wasn't comfortable having the upper hand. From now on I'll dominate you in ways you don't realize.
jokes.setup: Milhouse forgiving Bart
jokes.punchline: Reveals plan for subtle psychological manipulation
jokes.explanation: Excellent character development - shows Milhouse learning manipulation while staying true to his character

jokes.text: I can't even find my car in the parking lot at the mall. There was half a kilo of heroin and two suspects inside.
jokes.setup: Wiggum can't help find lost rag
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of losing serious criminal evidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Wiggum moment - escalates from personal incompetence to professional negligence casually

jokes.text: Men's extra large underwear? I'm saving my strength. He's going bike riding today.
jokes.setup: Rag asking other laundry to narrate
jokes.punchline: Underwear's strategic energy conservation for anticipated workout
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist concept of clothing having work awareness and energy management

jokes.text: Your bar is the closest thing this town has to an Algonquin roundtable. What about that Round Table Pizza run by that Algonquin guy? Yeah, yeah, okay, second closest.
jokes.setup: Marge comparing Moe's bar to literary salon
jokes.punchline: Homer's literal interpretation finding actual Algonquin-run pizza place
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic moment with perfect setup/punchline structure and character consistency

jokes.text: Well, all marriages have their ups and downs.
jokes.setup: Rag experiencing washing machine cycle
jokes.punchline: Treating mechanical agitation as marital relationship advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect closing joke that works on multiple levels - literal washing cycle and relationship metaphor

tmdb_overview: Moe's bar rag explains that he started as a medieval tapestry a thousand years earlier; Bart tries to reconcile with Milhouse; the disappearance of Moe's bar rag makes him realize he has friends.
